channel.title: Management on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Management on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Taking Responsibility As A Lead
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about taking responsibility as a lead.

If you’re wondering what I mean by lead, I have a whole video on game development roles. I’ll link it below, and I even have a very specific video on roles if you’re in the programming department, because a lot of the examples I’m going to give when I talk about this are going to be programming examples where I was or was not a lead programmer. I also have videos if you’re interested. There’s also videos on how you might want to uh, behave if you’re a lead for the first time and also the important importance of being consistent if you’re a lead. I’ll link those videos below as well. I hope they help.

item.title: Running Meetings
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to talk about running meetings, something I know you all love.

Everyone’s always telling me, Tim, I love meetings, Talk more about them. Actually, I know you don’t like meetings, but I was having lunch with a friend of mine who’s also in the game industry, in a very different role than I have. And I mentioned to her that last week I was in a meeting and I got called out for not talking at all. We got to the end of the meeting — It was almost an hour long — and somebody said: “Well, Tim, you’ve been quiet, You haven’t said anything. What do you think”, and I even said my answer was: “Well, this is the first time I’ve been to this meeting that you guys have been having for a few months now. I just wanted to listen”.

item.title: Institutional Knowledge
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about institutional knowledge, which is sometimes called tribal knowledge, but I don’t like that name.

Tribal knowledge It just it doesn’t really capture what I’m trying to say here. And institutional knowledge I will define as things you’re expected to know when you work at a particular company and on particular projects, but they’re not written down. Uh, I was. I had been planning this talk for about a week when I put up a video called RPG features. Nobody asked for, and one of the commenters, Scorbus, mentioned this very thing about: hey, do you think some of these RPG features keep getting put in because of loss of institutional knowledge? And I said yes, they do. And in fact I’m planning to talk about it. So let’s talk about it here [snorts]. And before I even start, let me just point out that that institutional knowledge is different from like preservation. Even I’m talking about like.

item.title: Fungible Employees
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I’m going to talk about flem.

You know what flem is in the back of your throat. Some of you are swallowing it right now. Today I want to do a deep dive into the nature and chemistry of flem. So let’s start with: Oh wait, no, no.

Today I want to talk about funible employees: Fungeible. I like that word because it’s got the word fun in it And a lot of companies think employees are funible. What do we mean by the word fungeible? And I like it. I’m using it here in a very specific way.

item.title: Leading For The First Time
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about leading for the first time, In particular, the very first time you’re put in a lead role or even leading an entire project, a game director role.

I want to talk about that because it’s scary, I know. But one thing I want you to remember is that everyone did it for the first time And I thought about the first time I was in charge of an entire project. And this was a long time ago. This was in 1994., and I had been a lead programmer before And I’d been a lead programmer when there weren’t any other programmers. So there was no management involved, It was just I’m the only programmer and I did all the stuff.

item.title: Prioritizing Tasks
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about prioritizing tasks.

What do I mean by this? Doesn’t matter, I think, whether you’re in a big, you know hundred thousand person team, you’re in a small team or even if you’re doing solo dev, you have to prioritize the work tasks to get a game done — And I realize I’ve never really talked about this, Um it. Some of it is, I always thought, kind of self-evident, but there is some minutia that’s kind of interesting and some stuff I’ve learned after a long time. So I thought I’d talk about prioritizing tasks And one of the first questions is: why do I have to do that? Just get it all done, because you will not get it all done.

item.title: Game Development As A Family Car Trip
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about game development.

As a family car trip, Bear with me on this. I’ve done a lot of talking about game development over the last couple years. I’ve talked about teams and all, how to organize them and how to look for good team members. I’ve talked about game directors and how to be a good one and pitfalls of being a game director. I’ve talked about design pillars. I’ve talked about, I’ve even dived in like intent, like this is people’s intent in making a game as opposed to people’s interpretation. I’ve even tried to explain how design choices are, even made at the most fundamental level.

item.title: The Blame Problem
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the blame problem.

I have a video called the credit problem And this video is the opposite. Let’s call it a companion video, so in the credit problem which I’m link below, and I I really, really recommend you watch it. But I’m going to have to summarize some things to put things into context, for this video I establish how hard it is to give credit for a feature or anything you’re trying to do credit for. It’s not easy at all And the problem is when people ask me: “Hey, hey, Tim, who did Feature X”. It means different things to different people.

item.title: Onboarding New Team Members
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about onboarding new team members.

This is from a question from pooa 8730 who asked looking at Team growth. In those photos they were talking about a video I posted of uh development during Fallout. I’ll put — I’ll link it below: how difficult was it to onboard new people? You need to get new people up to speed as fast as possible and they clearly have no knowledge of existing implemented things, especially in Game Dev where deadlines are super tight. Interesting question. This is another one of those questions where I never would have thought to do a video on this until someone asked, because getting people up to speed is just part of what you always did and so, like a fish surrounded by water, it didn’t occur to me that I was wet. But I do want to say this — and this isn’t uh directed to the person who made the comment, but just something I’ve noticed is onboarding. When I hear that term, it always makes me smile a little bit because it’s one of those terms that have snuck into game development from other places and it makes me realize how much influence is coming in from these places. We never used to use onboarding as a term. In fact, I don’t even think I heard that term before 10 years ago, and I certainly didn’t hear it frequently until about 5 years ago — and suddenly everything’s about or onboarding.

item.title: Communication Between Roles
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about communication between roles, specifically, how to handle all the different roles of people on your development team and getting them to talk to each other.

This is actually a question from Boku, who asks, I wonder, about the different communication channels between roles, which roles are often together and such because they are closely related into velopment — you hinted at level designers and narratives being together, for example. Boku, this is a really good question. It’s also a really serious question because it has to do with how you’re going to manage a team, especially as teams get bigger. This gets more and more important — and get them to work together. Even though there are several of them, many of different types of people. Some people want to have these discussions, some people will dominate the discussions, other people don’t want to have discussions at all, but you need to get people to communicate, and at the high level. I would just answer and say this: communic, crossr communication.

item.title: Matching Your Project And Team
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about matching your project and your team.

This is a topic I’ve wanted to talk about for a long time, but I’ve put it off because I know I’ve talked a lot about nuance and context on this channel, but I was trying to figure out a way of approaching this where people didn’t immediately think I was denigrating the team or any of the teams I’d worked with, despite all the wonderful things I’ve said. I know people will just jump there and go. He’s putting down the team, so let me just say this isn’t about the people. This is about a reality that I’ve talked about at length and it’s true for me, it’s true for you, it’s true for everybody.

item.title: Authority Without Responsibility
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Authority without responsibility.

Now I’m going to explain what I mean, but this is sort of a response to two of my own videos that have become very popular — my caution videos — and I will link them below where I talk about my concern that there’s a problem in the game industry: that developers are much too C cautious. They don’t spend time exploring different feature ideas. They overestimate their time on things. They want to polish things early.

item.title: Morale Improvement
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about morale Improvement.

Specifically, I want to answer a question from rhinosaur 77 who asked: I’d love to see a video on how you would keep team morale up during stressful times at work. The chocolate tasty meetings you had were great and the bacon Ferry was a highlight during work. Even if the fun police didn’t like it, really helped us keep a bit more sane. So I’ve never done a video on morale, um, and I have a couple caveats about that. But let me just mentioned that rhinosaurus 77 and I work together at carbine, so he’s somebody who knows me and he’s also a master Channel member. So I’m filming this on a Thursday morning. Since Friday’s already unlocked, this will be Monday’s video anyway.

item.title: The Business Side Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the business side of game development.

This is an answer to a question by Seth, the guy GD, who asks: I was wondering if you could explain the business side of the game industry. I know you started troa. I would love to hear the business stuff about it I know you have a video about troa, but could you go further into the money aspect of it? Could you explain starting up a studio and dealing with hiring people, choosing and approaching Publishers, getting funding for the game, Insurance, banking, PR, office space costs, advertising and other things people wouldn’t think of?

item.title: How To Remedy Problematic Behavior
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how to remedy problematic behaviors and these are on people on a team you’re on, in fact.

I’m going to talk about, kind of what kind of problematic behaviors you may encounter, what to do when you’re peer level with them, what to do if you’re above them. This actually comes from a uh view uh commenter and actually a channel member. Father Al win asks — you have Loosely discussed this prior, but when working within a team, what practices and habits that certain devs demonstrate do you consider problematic and how do you go about remedying them as both an equal team member and as a team lead? Okay, father aldwin, that’s a good question and because you are a channel member, it is being bumped up into the queue. So you asked on Saturday. I’m filming this on Sunday. Monday and Tuesdays have already unlocked for the master members, so this will be Wednesdays. Let’s get to it, first, let me Define problematic Behavior. It’s really hard for me to Define it. Um, it’s one of those things that you know. It’s like that definition of porn: it’s hard to Define, but I know it when I see it.

item.title: Working In Teams
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about working in teams — something you will almost inevitably have to do if you go in the game industry — and I want to tell you why I’m talking about this.

So last night, poking around Netflix, I found a Duran Duran documentary. Now, I was really big in Duran Duran in the 80s and then they made this comeback in the 90s and it was interesting to watch the history of it.

item.title: Relinquishing Creative Control
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about relinquishing creative control.

I’m going to answer a question from mjy media who asks: hey, Tim question, do you have tips on relinquishing creative control? I wrote a ttrpg book a couple years ago and handing off my writing to artists was the hardest thing to do. I had so many notes but I didn’t want to smother the artists. Some artist art didn’t turn out true to my vision and I’m so somewhat unhappy with around 20% of the art. I’m writing another book Now. Tips for this next round.

item.title: Delegation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about delegation, the dreaded delegation.

Actually. This is an answer to a question from future Tech pilot who asks: would you consider talking about delegation and what amount of power other people are comfortable with receiving, like I’m sure you’ve assigned a task but had to step in when the job wasn’t done correctly? What is the D Dynamic of that interaction between boss and employee?

item.title: Design: Committee vs. Collaboration
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about designed by committee versus design by collaboration.

In fact, I want to answer a question by cybercop 0083 who asks: you are critical of games by committee. Can you draw an approximate line between a committee product and a collaborative one? Yes, I can and yes, I am very critical of design by committee because I’ve never. I’ve never seen a committee make a good product. Um, and I’m not even talking games, but every good group that I’ve worked in they’ve done better.

item.title: Troika's Flat Hierarchy Experiment
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about flat management hierarchies.

I don’t think many of you realized that we did that at tra, or I should say we experimented with that at troa. It only lasted for one project, but Arcanum was created under a completely flat hierarchy. Yes, the we had. We had three owners and then all the employees. But I’ll explain. I’m going to go through exactly what I mean by flat hierarchy, but also part of the reason we had to have owners that there are legal reasons you have. You have to have someone who signs the paperwork with the lawyer, or signs the paperwork with the City of Irvine, or sign the paperwork with the county of orangee or the state of California or the federal government. You had to have someone who said, yeah, I’m in charge here for some things external to the company, internal. We were about as flat as you can be and I’ll walk through and explain. So this was. Troa from 98 to 2001. We hired only seniors.

item.title: My Biggest Lesson
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to answer a question about lessons.

Zamala asks wondering what Tim’s self-editing answer with some background, because I want to talk about working with teams, and this isn’t what you think it’s going to be. I actually like working with teams, and I think that goes back to. I loved I know I started playing D and D in high school. That was fun. I worked with teams in even in elementary school. I talked about in one video trying to do a New York Times crossword puzzle for a whole week in a reading class. I like that, then I went to engineering school where you’re put on project classes. It’s just part of the curriculum so you learn how to do that. I know a lot of people, especially in high school and college. When you’re assigned to project classes and you’re given a team, it can be a miserable experience because there are some people out there that just really try.

item.title: Crunch
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about crunch.

Been a lot of people saying a lot of things about crunch. People been saying a lot of things about crunch for decades. Uh, I don’t know how much I can add to this other than my own experience with crunch, in both being on crunch that was imposed on me, being on crutches that I imposed, being on crunches that were caused by other people being on crunches at high cost. So it gives me a pretty big perspective on crunch. And let me just Define crunch first of all. Crunch is the extra work usually at the end of a game, but it could occur before making a demo or a conference. It’s a crunch that happens when the game’s development Gets behind schedule. So you have to work hard — extra hours, weekends — to make up and get the game back on schedule now to me, one of the most annoying things I hear, and I’m going to be very careful how I say this. I hear this a lot. Quote: crunch is always the result of bad management. Unquote.

item.title: The Credits Problem (e.g. Fallout Companions)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about credits and how people get credited in games, and I’m going to do that by telling you a story, because I’m frequently asked: hey uh, who did the companions in Fallout?

I love the companions. Let me tell a story, so when we first started designing Fallout in 1994.. There was a meeting where we were talking about how to do some fundamental design work and it came up: are we going to be playing one character? Are we going to have a party?

item.title: How To Build A Motivated Team (1996)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today, I’m going to read to you a presentation that I made — I think it was sometime in 1996 — at interplay, on how to build a motivated team.

I mentioned this in a previous video. I didn’t have the presentation for years, I just had the notes I used to write it. But a few years ago an ex-co-worker from interplay approached me and he had a printed copy of my talk. So I have the actual thing that I presented, and I will. I I wrote a preface to it because this is written 27 years ago. I think I was 30 years old. It was my first and last time being a producer, so I thought this needed a little introduction. So I’ll read you the instruction and then I’ll do the presentation. So here we go, so sometime in late 96, Brian Fargo asked me to address the other producers in our regular producer meeting. He liked how I was running my Fallout project. And he said he was equally impressed with the high morale of the team. He asked me to talk about how I managed to do that when many of the other producers were complaining to him of low team morale and low work ethic. Bear in mind when reading this: I don’t consider myself to be a particularly good producer then or now, but the bar was low for good production and interplay, not because they, The Producers, weren’t trying, but because we had absolutely no training. In fact, when I asked the executive producer there for training, he said that’s not how they did things. They just threw people in the pool to see who could swim well. I didn’t think being held up as an example of a great producer was a good idea for me, but it was something I didn’t understand at the time and the document below will.
